buckets.payg: Personal/PAYG individual CONTEXT for spend (money out). NOT a deductibility claim — deductibility is decided later at year-end review.
buckets.company: Pty Ltd business expense (money out) — the company books of record.
buckets.property_rented: Expense (money out) on a currently-rented investment property.
buckets.property_vacant: Holding cost (money out) on a vacant/not-genuinely-available property.
buckets.income_business: Money IN (a credit) that is business revenue received by the Pty Ltd — sales, fees, invoices paid.
buckets.income_property: Money IN (a credit) that is rent received on an investment property (pair with the property).
buckets.income_personal: Money IN (a credit) that is personal income — salary/PAYG, interest, dividends received personally.
buckets.refund: Money IN (a credit) that REVERSES a prior expense (a refund/reversal) — nets against spend, it is NOT income.
buckets.asset: Money OUT (a debit) for a CAPITAL purchase — equipment/plant or a capital improvement that DEPRECIATES over its life. NOT an immediate deduction; it becomes a depreciating asset and is flagged for review. Use for clearly capital items (e.g. a laptop/computer, machinery, furniture, an appliance for a rental).

guidance: Categorise each line into exactly one bucket. A line marked money OUT (a debit / spend) takes an expense bucket (payg, company, property_*), or 'asset' if it is a clearly capital purchase that depreciates (large equipment, computers, machinery, furniture). A line marked money IN (a credit) takes an income bucket (income_business, income_property, income_personal) or 'refund' if it reverses an expense. Prefer 'unknown' over guessing — income and capital misclassification are costly. This is general information, not tax advice; never assert deductibility.

merchant_hints.note: Allied-health treatment (physio, dental, optical, psychology, etc.) is a private cost and generally not deductible. It may count toward your private-health EXTRAS limit — track it against your cover and capture amounts from your private-health statement, not as a deduction.

_asset_class_note: GENERAL-INFO effective-life defaults for auto-created depreciating assets. Approximations aligned with common ATO TR 2022/1 lives — a sensible starting point only. Auto-created assets still land needs_review for the user to confirm the class, effective life, method and business-use % with a registered tax agent.

claimability.general_info_note: Repairs that restore a rented property to working condition are generally deductible in the year paid (improvements are not).
A new appliance in a rented property is generally a depreciating asset (Div 40), claimed over its effective life — not immediately.
Holding costs are only deductible while the property is genuinely available for rent; vacant land holding costs are generally not deductible since 1 July 2019.
Car expenses on a novated lease are not deductible to the employee — the employer leases the car. The reportable fringe benefits amount (RFBA) is reportable.
Compulsory uniforms, union/professional fees and CPD self-education are commonly deductible for nurses. The initial cost of getting registered is not deductible.
Home-office running costs can be claimed at the ATO's fixed rate per hour worked from home; a work computer over $300 is depreciated rather than claimed immediately.

Rent you pay on your own home is generally a private expense and not deductible. Only a sole trader running a genuine 'place of business' from home can apportion occupancy costs — employees and remote workers generally cannot claim rent (only running costs).
Rent on business or commercial premises you lease is generally deductible. If the premises is partly private, only the business-use portion is claimable.
Commercial premises rent paid by your company is generally a deductible business expense. Apportion out any private use.

Additional running costs of working from home (electricity, internet, phone, stationery) can be claimed using the ATO's fixed-rate-per-hour method or the actual-cost method, for the hours you genuinely work from home. You cannot claim occupancy costs (rent, mortgage interest, rates) unless your home is a genuine place of business, and you must keep a record of your hours.

Self-education and professional-development costs are generally deductible when the study has a sufficient connection to your current income-earning work. Study to get a new job or enter a new field is generally not deductible, and apportionment is needed where there is private use.

claimability.merchant_hint: donation,red cross,rspca,salvation army,unicef,oxfam,smith family,beyond blue,cancer council,world vision,charity,dgr
claimability.general_info_note: Gifts and donations to an organisation endorsed as a deductible gift recipient (DGR) are generally deductible above the minimum-gift threshold. The gift must be genuine with nothing material received in return, and you should keep the receipt. Buying raffle tickets, chocolates or attendance at fundraising events is generally not a deductible gift.

Premiums for income-protection (salary-continuance) insurance are generally deductible where the policy covers loss of income, but only the income-protection portion held outside super. Premiums for life, trauma or total-and-permanent-disability cover, and cover held inside superannuation, are generally not deductible.

The cost of managing your tax affairs is generally deductible — including registered tax agent and accountant fees for preparing and lodging your return, ATO interest charges, and travel to obtain tax advice from a recognised adviser.

Annual union fees and subscriptions to trade, business or professional associations connected to your work are generally deductible. The joining fee or a portion attributable to non-work benefits may need to be excluded.

Teachers can generally claim work-related items they pay for themselves such as classroom supplies, teaching resources and the work-use portion of a phone or home internet. Excursion and camp costs you incur for work, and self-education that maintains or improves your teaching skills, may also be claimable. Everyday clothing is not deductible.

Ongoing renewals such as first-aid certificates and working-with-children checks required for your current teaching role are generally deductible. The cost of your initial teacher registration or qualification is not.

Tradespeople can generally claim protective clothing (steel-capped boots, hi-vis, sun protection used outdoors) and the cost of tools and equipment used for work. Tools above the immediate-deduction threshold are depreciated over their effective life rather than claimed immediately, and any private use is apportioned out.

A tradesperson carrying bulky tools or travelling between work sites may be able to claim work-related car expenses using the cents-per-kilometre or logbook method. Normal travel between home and a regular workplace is private and not claimable; keep a logbook or records to support the work-use percentage.

Healthcare workers can generally claim compulsory or logo'd uniforms and their laundry, AHPRA registration renewals, union or professional fees, and CPD self-education that relates to their current role. Plain everyday clothing and the initial cost of getting registered are not deductible.

Office professionals can generally claim the work-use portion of home-office running costs, phone and internet, professional memberships and work-related subscriptions. Equipment above the immediate-deduction threshold is depreciated rather than claimed immediately, and conventional business attire is not deductible.

Drivers who use their own vehicle for work can generally claim work-related car expenses using the cents-per-kilometre or logbook method (not both). Travel between home and a regular workplace is private. A logbook and odometer records are needed to support the actual-cost method.

Hospitality workers can generally claim compulsory or distinctive uniforms (such as chef's whites and logo'd aprons) and their laundry, plus tools of trade like a chef's knife set used for work. Renewals such as RSA and food-handling certificates needed for your current role may also be claimable; plain black-and-white clothing is not.

Sales professionals can generally claim the work-use portion of phone and car expenses for work travel and client visits, plus work-related subscriptions and memberships. Entertaining clients (meals, drinks, events) is generally not deductible, and home-to-work travel is private.

ADF members can generally claim their distinctive service uniform items and laundry, and work-specific equipment they paid for. Haircuts/grooming are not deductible even under appearance standards, and fitness costs are only claimable in narrow special-duty cases — confirm those with a registered tax agent.

Police officers can generally claim police association/union fees, their distinctive uniform and its laundry, and duty equipment they bought themselves. Plain-clothes/detective business attire is conventional clothing and not deductible even with a clothing allowance.

Aged and disability care workers can generally claim logo'd/occupation-specific uniforms and laundry, PPE (gloves, masks, non-slip shoes), first-aid/CPR renewals for the current role, and union fees. A police check or NDIS worker screening for a NEW job is a pre-employment cost and not deductible.

Cleaners can generally claim cleaning supplies and equipment they paid for (immediate write-off at or under $300; depreciated above), PPE and logo'd uniforms with laundry. Equipment or supplies the employer provides or reimburses can't be claimed.

Retail workers can generally claim a compulsory logo'd uniform and its laundry, and union fees (e.g. SDA). Plain clothing in the store's colours - or items from the store's own range you were told to wear - is conventional clothing and NOT deductible.

Real estate employees can generally claim their licence/registration RENEWAL (not the first licence to enter the industry), professional-institute fees, and marketing costs they genuinely bear themselves - agency cost-sharing splits are fact-specific. Suits and business attire are conventional clothing and not deductible.

Security guards can generally claim their licence RENEWAL (the FIRST licence to get the job is not deductible), distinctive uniform and laundry, protective equipment they bought, first-aid renewals and union fees. Plain dark clothing without logos is conventional clothing and not deductible.

Apprentices and trainees can generally claim tools they paid for (immediate at or under $300, depreciated above), protective clothing/PPE and union fees. Course/TAFE fees are only deductible where connected to the CURRENT apprenticeship and actually borne by you (government subsidies and employer reimbursements are not) - fee treatment is fact-specific.

thresholds_by_fy._note: All dollar thresholds in CENTS. These change yearly and are policy-dependent — the depreciation engine + claimability brain read them per FY, never inline. Confirm against ATO before relying on them.

_reference_only_keys._note: REFERENCE-ONLY: these per-FY keys are NOT used in any $ calculation in the engines. They are informational only — `div293_threshold_cents` informs a defer-to-agent readiness nudge (never a computed liability); `super_non_concessional_cap_cents` is informational only (bring-forward + total-super-balance conditions make any breach determination agent territory); `mls_single_threshold_cents` and the company-tax rates (`base_rate_company_tax_pct`/`full_company_tax_pct`) are unconsumed (the product never computes tax payable, by design — see CLAUDE.md); `div7a_benchmark_rate_pct` is unconsumed and re-verified each July when the ATO publishes the new rate; `franking_holding_rule_threshold_cents` (the $5,000 small-shareholder exemption boundary for the 45-day holding rule) and `fito_de_minimis_cents` (the $1,000 FITO de-minimis below which the offset-limit calculation isn't required) drive defer-to-agent readiness nudges only — holding periods and offset limits are never computed. Do not wire these into any position maths without a registered-tax-agent review; surcharge/levy/company-tax computation is out of scope.

payg_deductibility._note: DENY-BY-DEFAULT deductibility lists for the 'payg' (individual) bucket, used by src/lib/deductibility.ts to stamp a capture state at ingest. GENERAL INFO ONLY — never tax advice; never asserts a $ figure. Matched (case-insensitive substring) against the line's ato_label + merchant. Precedence: deny → apportion → undetermined. The matcher only DENIES private spend or flags APPORTIONMENT; it never auto-asserts a positive deduction — clearly-deductible payg spend (union fees, tax-affairs, donations) stays 'undetermined', is excluded by deny-by-default, and is surfaced for the user to confirm (so resolved_deductible_cents stays ~$0 until a real review). Company spend (separate taxpayer) and capital 'asset' spend are excluded by BUCKET, not here. Deductible claim guidance for these categories lives in the 'claimability' rules above.

deny.match: private-health,private health,health:private-insurance,bupa,medibank,hcf,gmhba,hbf,frank health,australian unity health,life insurance,life-insurance,insurance:life,tpd,trauma,total and permanent disability,funeral insurance
deny.note: Private health insurance and life/TPD/trauma/funeral cover are private and generally not deductible. Private health insurance still matters for tax — it drives the private health rebate and the Medicare Levy Surcharge — but that's captured from your private health statement, not as a deduction. (Income-protection cover held outside super is treated separately and may be deductible.)

Allied-health and dental/optical treatment is a private cost and generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)). It may count toward your private-health extras limit — track it against your cover, captured from your private-health statement, not as a deduction.
Groceries and ordinary food are private/domestic spend — generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)).
Private or domestic living costs are generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)).

Personal loan/credit-card repayments, fees and interest are private or capital in nature — generally not deductible. Only the principal-free interest on a loan used to earn income is deductible, which is captured separately.
Meals and entertainment are generally not deductible (Division 32 / s8-1(2)(b)), even with a work connection, unless a specific exception applies.
The cost of your ordinary daily meals is a private expense and generally not deductible.
Gym, fitness and general health costs are private and generally not deductible (narrow occupation exceptions exist — confirm with a registered tax agent).
Conventional everyday clothing (including swimwear) is private and not deductible (TR 97/12). Only compulsory/occupation-specific or protective clothing is claimable.
Flowers are a private/personal expense and generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)).

Raffle tickets, art-union/prize-home entries and lotteries are not tax-deductible gifts — even when a charity runs them — because you receive a chance to win (a material benefit). A genuine donation of $2 or more to a DGR with nothing in return is claimable instead.

apportion.note: Additional running costs of working from home can be claimed at the ATO's fixed rate per hour (PCG 2023/1) or the actual-cost method for the hours you genuinely work from home — the fixed rate already bundles energy, internet, phone and stationery, so don't also claim those items separately. Needs your hours before it can be claimed.
Self-education is deductible only where the study has a sufficient connection to your CURRENT income-earning work (TR 2024/3). Study to get a new job is not. Needs apportionment for any private use.

Work-related car/travel may be claimed by the cents-per-kilometre (a set ATO rate per km, capped at 5,000 work km per car per year) or logbook method. Travel between home and a regular workplace is private. Needs your work-use portion before it can be claimed.

allow_suggest.note: Union fees and professional-association subscriptions directly related to earning your income are generally deductible (s8-1). Confirm the amount and that it relates to your work, then mark it as a deduction.
The cost of managing your tax affairs (registered tax agent fees) is generally deductible (s25-5). Confirm it relates to your tax affairs, then mark it as a deduction.

allow_suggest.match: donation,donations,charity,dgr,gift-deductible,red cross,rspca,unicef,salvation army,salvos,smith family,vinnies,st vincent de paul,oxfam,world vision,cancer council,starlight,beyond blue,fred hollows,medecins sans frontieres,msf australia,guide dogs,royal flying doctor,wires,lifeline
allow_suggest.note: Gifts of $2 or more to a deductible gift recipient (DGR) are generally deductible (Div 30). Confirm the organisation is a DGR and you received no material benefit, then mark it as a deduction.

Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super) are generally deductible because the benefit would be assessable income (s8-1). Confirm it's not a life/TPD/trauma policy, then mark it as a deduction.

occupations._note: #143: occupation deduction content keyed by income_activities.occupation_scope. 'suggest' items become claim_suggestions (suggested_deductible — never auto-counted; deny-by-default holds). 'warn' items pre-empt the ATO's top occupation errors. GENERAL INFO ONLY.

nurse.label: Nurse / midwife
suggest: AHPRA registration renewal (required to practise) is generally deductible.
Nursing/professional association and union fees related to your work are generally deductible.
Compulsory or occupation-specific uniform and laundry (non-slip nursing shoes, logo'd scrubs).
Self-education directly connected to your CURRENT nursing role (e.g. a wound-care course).
Work-specific equipment: fob watch, stethoscope, safety glasses, gloves.
Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super).
warn: Plain black pants/shoes and ordinary clothing are conventional clothing — NOT deductible even if worn only at work.
The $150 laundry figure is a substantiation threshold, not an automatic entitlement.
Self-education to move into a NEW role (e.g. carer → RN) is not deductible.
You can't claim home-to-work travel, or anything your employer reimbursed.

tradesperson.label: Tradesperson
suggest: Tools and equipment: immediate write-off ≤$300; depreciate >$300 (a tool SET >$300 is depreciated even if each piece is <$300).
Protective clothing/PPE: steel-cap boots, hi-vis, hard hats, safety glasses, sun protection for outdoor work.
Compulsory or logo'd uniforms and laundry.
Union fees and self-education for your current trade.
Car between sites via cents-per-km or logbook (not home-to-work).
warn: Conventional clothing (plain jeans, drill shirts) is NOT deductible even if only worn at work.
Home-to-work commuting is private and not deductible.
Declare cash-job income — it's assessable.
Don't claim a tool set's components individually to dodge the $300 threshold.

teacher.label: Teacher
suggest: Teaching registration and professional association/union fees.
Self-education connected to your current teaching role.
Classroom supplies and resources you paid for yourself.
Excursion costs you incurred and weren't reimbursed for.
Work-related portion of phone/internet and home-office running costs.
warn: Everyday clothing isn't deductible (only compulsory/logo'd or protective items).
Home-to-school travel is private.
Anything the school reimbursed can't be claimed.

it_professional.label: IT professional
suggest: Work-related portion of home-office running costs (the ATO fixed rate per hour).
Self-education and certifications relevant to your current role.
Professional association/union fees.
Depreciation of self-bought work equipment (laptop, monitor) for the work-use portion.
Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super).
warn: Don't double-dip phone/internet on top of the WFH fixed rate — it already bundles them.
An employer-supplied laptop isn't yours to depreciate.
Home-to-work travel is private.
Conventional clothing and business attire are private — not deductible.

healthcare_worker.label: Healthcare worker
suggest: Professional registration renewals (e.g. AHPRA) required for your current role.
Occupation-specific clothing (scrubs, logo'd polos) and its laundry; non-slip work shoes.
PPE you paid for: gloves, masks, protective glasses.
Self-education directly connected to your CURRENT role.
Professional association and union fees.
Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super).
warn: Conventional clothing (plain pants, everyday shoes) is NOT deductible even if worn only at work.
Home-to-work travel is private; travel BETWEEN workplaces can be claimable.
Anything your employer reimbursed can't be claimed.
Grooming and personal-care costs are private.

office_professional.label: Office professional
suggest: Work-related portion of home-office running costs (the ATO fixed rate per hour, with a record of hours).
Professional memberships, subscriptions and union fees tied to your work.
Self-education connected to your CURRENT role.
Depreciation of self-bought work equipment (laptop, monitor) for the work-use portion.
Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super).
warn: Business attire (suits, corporate wear) is conventional clothing — NOT deductible even if your employer requires it.
Coffee, lunches and commuting are private.
Don't claim phone/internet separately on top of the WFH fixed rate — it already bundles them.
An employer-supplied laptop isn't yours to depreciate.

driver.label: Driver (rideshare / delivery / truck)
suggest: Car expenses via a 12-week logbook (typical for rideshare) or cents-per-km — one method per car.
Platform commissions and fees charged against your fares/deliveries.
Work-use portion of your phone and phone mounts/chargers.
Cleaning costs for a car used for ride-sourcing.
Safety gear and hi-vis for delivery/truck work.
warn: You can't claim fuel, rego or servicing ON TOP of the cents-per-km rate — it already covers them.
Travel from home to your first job/pickup is generally private.
Taxi and ride-sourcing drivers must register for GST from the FIRST dollar of fares — the usual turnover threshold doesn't apply.
Fines and penalties are never deductible.

hospitality_worker.label: Hospitality worker
suggest: Chef's whites, logo'd aprons and other compulsory/distinctive uniform items, plus laundry.
Tools of trade you paid for (e.g. a chef's knife set — depreciated if the set cost over $300).
RSA, RCG and food-safety certificate RENEWALS needed for your current role.
Union fees.
warn: Plain black-and-white clothing is conventional clothing — NOT deductible even if the venue requires it.
Your own meals and coffees during shifts are private.
Home-to-work travel is private, including late-night shifts.
The FIRST RSA/certificate that got you the job is a pre-employment cost, not deductible.

sales_professional.label: Sales professional
suggest: Car expenses for client visits and between-site travel (cents-per-km or logbook) — not home-to-work.
Work-use portion of phone and internet.
Professional subscriptions, memberships and union fees.
Self-education connected to your CURRENT role.
Work-related travel (fares, accommodation) with records.
warn: Suits and business attire are conventional clothing — NOT deductible.
Entertaining clients (meals, drinks, events) is generally NOT deductible (Division 32).
Home-to-work travel is private.
Grooming and personal presentation costs are private.

adf_member.label: ADF member
suggest: Distinctive service uniform items you paid for (camouflage, mess dress, service shirts) and their laundry.
Work-specific equipment you bought and weren't reimbursed for.
Self-education directly connected to your CURRENT service role.
Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super).
warn: Haircuts and grooming are NOT deductible — even under military appearance standards.
Gym and fitness costs are generally private; only narrow special-duty cases differ — confirm with a registered tax agent.
Home-to-base travel is private.
Mess subscriptions and social functions are private.

police_officer.label: Police officer
suggest: Police association / union fees.
Distinctive uniform items and their laundry/dry-cleaning.
Duty equipment you bought yourself (torch, holsters, utility belt items).
Self-education connected to your CURRENT policing role.
Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super).
warn: Plain-clothes/detective business attire is conventional clothing — NOT deductible even with a clothing allowance.
Haircuts and grooming are NOT deductible despite appearance standards.
Gym and fitness costs are generally private; narrow exceptions for specialist tactical roles — confirm with a registered tax agent.
Home-to-station travel is private.

aged_care_worker.label: Aged & disability care worker
suggest: Logo'd or occupation-specific uniform and its laundry; non-slip shoes.
PPE you paid for: gloves, masks, aprons, hand sanitiser for client work.
First-aid, CPR and manual-handling RENEWALS for your current role.
Union fees.
Car travel BETWEEN clients or facilities during your shift (not home-to-work).
warn: Conventional clothing is NOT deductible even if you only wear it to work.
Home to your FIRST client and home from your last is generally private.
A police check or NDIS worker screening for a NEW job is a pre-employment cost — not deductible.
Anything your employer reimbursed can't be claimed.

cleaner.label: Cleaner
suggest: Cleaning supplies and equipment you paid for (immediate write-off at or under $300; depreciated above).
PPE: gloves, masks, non-slip shoes.
Logo'd uniform and its laundry.
Car travel BETWEEN cleaning jobs/sites (not home-to-work).
Union fees.
warn: Conventional clothing is NOT deductible even if it only gets worn (and ruined) at work.
Home to your first site is generally private.
Supplies and equipment your employer provides or reimburses can't be claimed.
Declare cash-job income — it's assessable.

retail_worker.label: Retail worker
suggest: A compulsory, logo'd/distinctive uniform and its laundry.
Union fees (e.g. SDA).
Self-education connected to your CURRENT role.
Work-use portion of your phone where your role genuinely requires it.
warn: Plain clothing in the store's colours — or items from the store's own range you were told to wear — is conventional clothing and NOT deductible.
Buying the products you sell is private spending.
Makeup and grooming are NOT deductible even where the store requires a presented look.
Home-to-work travel is private.

real_estate_agent.label: Real estate agent
suggest: Licence/registration RENEWAL and professional-institute fees (REIV/REIQ etc.).
Car expenses for inspections and client visits via a logbook (usually the better method for high work km).
Work-use portion of phone and internet.
Marketing and advertising costs you genuinely bear yourself (not the vendor's or agency's share).
Self-education connected to your CURRENT role.
warn: Suits and business attire are conventional clothing — NOT deductible even though presentation matters in the job.
Your FIRST licence/certificate of registration (to enter the industry) is not deductible.
Grooming is private.
Home-to-office travel is private; travel between inspections during the day is claimable with records.
Client entertainment and gifts have strict limits (Division 32) — confirm with a registered tax agent.

security_guard.label: Security guard
suggest: Security licence RENEWAL (required to keep working).
Distinctive/logo'd uniform and its laundry.
Protective equipment you bought: torch, duty boots, hi-vis.
First-aid renewals and union fees.
Car travel BETWEEN sites during a shift (not home-to-work).
warn: Your FIRST security licence (to get the job) is a pre-employment cost — not deductible.
Plain dark clothing without logos is conventional clothing and NOT deductible.
Gym and fitness costs are private.
Home-to-site travel is private.

apprentice.label: Apprentice / trainee
suggest: Tools you paid for: immediate write-off at or under $300, depreciated above (a tool SET over $300 is depreciated even if each piece is under).
Protective clothing/PPE: steel-caps, hi-vis, safety glasses.
Union fees.
Course fees connected to your CURRENT apprenticeship that YOU actually paid (not subsidised or reimbursed) — treatment is fact-specific, confirm with a registered tax agent.
warn: Tools your employer supplied or reimbursed can't be claimed.
Home-to-work travel is private (unless you're carrying bulky tools with no secure storage at work — keep evidence).
Study to qualify for a DIFFERENT job is not deductible.
Government incentives and allowances aren't deductions — and they may be assessable income.
